summary: Gruber argues that Substack has created a deliberate branding trap that subsumes individual writers' identities under the Substack brand. He identifies three core problems: the revenue split, the false promise of audience-building network effects, and the Nazi content issue. Using Paul Krugman as a case study, he shows how even a Nobel Prize-winning columnist gets reduced to someone who 'writes for Substack' rather than being recognized as an independent publisher. Gruber contends that established writers from prestige outlets gravitate to Substack seeking a badge of legitimacy, but the platform offers only faux prestige while capturing their brand equity. He contrasts Substack with Ghost, WordPress, and other CMSes where no one conflates the tool with the publication.
key_points: Substack has successfully gotten people to call publications 'substacks' rather than blogs or newsletters, a branding coup that subordinates creators to the platform
Writers with established reputations joining Substack are building Substack's brand, not their own — the supposed network effects are unproven
Older writers from prestige outlets like the NYT or ABC News may find comfort under Substack's auspices, but the perceived legitimacy is illusory
No one says writers 'write for WordPress' or 'write for Ghost' — only Substack has achieved this conflation of CMS with publisher, and it's by design
Krugman's independent writing is vastly better than his later NYT work, making it all the more unfortunate that his brand is being captured by Substack
money_quotes: Writers with established reputations and readerships joining Substack are helping build Substack's brand, not their own.
Reading Krugman this year, on his own site, has been like rediscovering cane sugar Coca-Cola after drinking the cheaper-to-produce corn syrup variant for a few years.
Krugman hung out his independent shingle at Substack — which makes it a shingle under a shingle.
There's no real prestige at Substack and never will be.
No one would say that Jason Snell 'writes for WordPress', or that Jason Kottke or yours truly 'writes for Movable Type'.
Only with Substack does anyone perceive creator branding as being subservient to the platform — something that ought to be seen merely as an interchangeable CMS — like that.
That is not by happenstance. It's a trap and it is by design. It's exactly what Substack wants, and exactly what independent writers and content producers should not.
key_insight: Substack has engineered a branding trap where independent writers' identities become subordinate to the platform, turning what should be an interchangeable CMS into a perceived publisher — and that conflation serves Substack's interests at the direct expense of the creators it hosts.
